data.title: Alcoa plunges as LME aluminum futures post biggest drop since 2018
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) plummeted 11% in early trading Thursday while aluminum futures on the London Metal Exchange fell by the most since 2018, as rising worries about the global economic impact of the war in Iran caused broad losses across industrial metals markets.

LME benchmark three-month aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) fell as much as 8% to $3,115/ton before bouncing to $3,214/ton, still wiping out gains that had piled up since the start of the Middle East war that had been fueled by risks to regional aluminum supplies.

Still, "while buyers across Asia, Europe, and the U.S. are scrambling to secure aluminum, stockpiles in China continue to build, tempering some of the bullish price pressures," ANZ analysts said in a note, adding that higher aluminum prices outside of China are incentivizing producers there to export more, which mitigates some of the global supply risks.

The Middle East accounts for 8%-9% of global aluminum output, but produces only 3% of global alumina and 1% of bauxite, leaving smelters highly dependent on seaborne imports, analysts say.

Among other potentially relevant stocks: Century Aluminum (CENX) down 8.2%, Constellium (CSTM) down 4.6%, Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) down 3.9%, Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) down 3.5%.

data.title: Federal Reserve formally proposes new bank capital, G-SIB rules
data.description:
The Federal Reserve proposed three new capital rules for banks, seeking to make the regulatory regime more efficient and keep U.S. banks competitive, Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman said on Thursday.

Two of the proposals apply to the U.S.'s largest banks — the Basel III framework and the Global Systemically Important Bank ((G-SIB)) surcharge. These "streamline the risk-based capital framework using a single set of calculations, improve alignment between requirements and risk, and revise the G-SIB surcharge to better capture the risks of our largest and most complex banks," she said.

The third proposal updates capital requirements for smaller and less complex banks to better align requirements with risks of traditional lending.

The central bank, in its role of supervising banks, worked with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. to rework the capital and G-SIB proposals.

"Together, these changes would strengthen our overall capital framework, which would remain robust under the new regime," Bowman said. The vice chair had been a vocal critic of an earlier proposal that would have significantly increased capital requirements for the country's largest banks, saying that they "over-calibrated" the rules when compared with regulations in other parts of the world.

In describing the latest proposal, she said, "This proposal makes targeted, reasonable changes to better calibrate requirements based on risk. These changes will continue to promote safety and soundness and U.S. financial stability."

Bowman had previewed the proposed rules last week. Today's official introduction of the new regimes didn't much help bank stocks. The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index (BKX) slipped 1.0% in late morning trading on Thursday as most of the U.S. megabanks traded in the red. JPMorgan Chase (JPM) fell 0.7%, Morgan Stanley (MS) slipped 0.7%, Bank of America (BAC) dipped 0.9%, Wells Fargo (WFC) dropped 1.3%, and Goldman Sachs (GS) was off 1.0%. Citigroup (C) edged up 0.2%.

data.title: LivaNova gains FDA premarket approval for aura6000 for sleep apnea
data.description:
• The US FDA has granted premarket approval to LivaNova (LIVN) for its obstructive sleep apnea system aura6000.
  • Aura6000 is designed for individuals with an apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) between 15 and 65 who have failed positive airway pressure (PAP), the traditional standard treatment for sleep apnea.
  • LivaNova's device uses proximal hypoglossal nerve stimulation, a different neurostimulation mechanism of action, from PAP devices.
  • Pending further FDA review, aura6000 should become available in H1 2027.
  • Aura6000 would likely compete with Inspire Medical Systems' (INSP) devices, which also utilize proximal hypoglossal nerve stimulation.

data.title: Accenture rises after Q2 beat, revises outlook
data.description:
Shares of Accenture (ACN) rose about 5% on Thursday after fiscal second quarter results beat estimates, despite third quarter revenue coming in below expectations.

The company also raised the lower end of its full fiscal year 2026 revenue and adjusted EPS outlook.

Q2 Metrics 

For the fiscal second quarter ended Feb. 28, revenue rose 8% in U.S. dollar terms (4% in local currency) year-over-year to $18.04B, while GAAP EPS climbed about 4% year-on-year to $2.93. Both top and bottom line numbers surpassed analysts' expectations.

"We delivered record second quarter bookings of $22.1 billion, including a record 41 clients with quarterly bookings greater than $100 million, with revenues at the top of our guided range, while continuing to take significant share in a competitive market," said Accenture's Chair and CEO Julie Sweet.

The company said new bookings for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 were $22.11B, an increase of 6% in U.S. dollars and 1% in local currency compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2025.

The Irish company's Consulting revenues in the first quarter grew 7% (in U.S. dollars) year-over-year to $8.86B, while Managed Services revenues jumped 10% (in U.S. dollars) to $9.18B.

Americas was the top geographic market by revenue, generating $8.90B in the fiscal second quarter, growing 4% (in U.S. dollars) year-over-year.

The company had a free cash flow of $3.7B.

Dividend: Accenture said it has declared a quarterly cash dividend of $1.63 per share for shareholders of record at the close of business on April 9. This dividend, which is payable on May 15, represents a 10% increase over the quarterly dividend rate of $1.48 per share in fiscal 2025.

Stock Buyback: During the second quarter of fiscal 2026, Accenture bought back or redeemed 6.8M shares for a total of $1.7B, including 5.1M shares repurchased in the open market. Accenture’s total remaining share repurchase authority at Feb. 28 was about $4.4B.

Outlook

Accenture said its third-quarter and full-year 2026 business outlook reflect the company’s best view of the potential impact of the conflict in the Middle East in the second half of this fiscal year. It does not take into account a significant escalation or the occurrence of major economic disruption.

The company expects fiscal third quarter revenue to be between $18.35B and $19B (midpoint at $18.675B), versus a consensus revenue estimate of $18.73B. 

The guidance assumes a foreign exchange impact of about +2.5% versus the third quarter of fiscal 2025. It implies 1% to 5% local-currency growth, including about a 1% impact from federal business. Excluding federal, a 2% to 6% local-currency growth.

For the full-year fiscal 2026, Accenture now expects revenue growth (in local currency) to be in the range of 3% to 5%, versus a prior forecast of 2% to 5%. The guidance includes an estimated 1% impact from federal business; excluding federal, 4% to 6% local-currency growth.

The foreign exchange impact on results in USD is expected to be about +2% versus fiscal year 2025. 

The company now expects adjusted EPS to be between $13.65 and $13.90 (midpoint at $13.775) versus a prior forecast of $13.52 and $13.90 (midpoint at $13.71), compared to a consensus EPS estimate of $13.86.

Acquisitions: The company now expects to invest about $5B in acquisitions, with the capacity to do more if opportunities arise.

Accenture raised fiscal 2026 free cash flow guidance by $1B. The company now expects free cash flow to be between $10.8B and $11.5B, versus a prior forecast of $9.8B to $10.5B, reflecting a free cash flow to net income ratio of 1.3.

Full-year 2026 adjusted operating margin is expected to be 15.7% to 15.9%, representing 10 bps to 30 bps expansion over adjusted FY 2025 results.

The company said the fiscal year 2026 operating cash flow expected range is $11.5B to $12.2B.

Fiscal year 2026 property and equipment additions are now expected to be about $700M.

Accenture continues to expect to return at least $9.3B through dividends and share repurchases, an increase of $1B, or 12%, from fiscal year 2025.

Analyst Commentary

Jefferies kept its Hold rating and $215 price target on Accenture's stock.

Analysts led by Surinder Thind said, "results once again came in near the top end of mgmt's [management's] guidance range and beat expectations across both revenue and adj. EPS. However, questions about the resiliency of demand are unlikely to go away, which has been modestly positive but stable."

The analysts added that not helping is limited bookings growth of +1% year-over-year in constant currency, or cc, in what is typically the strongest quarter of the year. The analysts noted that year-over-year cc revenue growth slowed for the fourth quarter in a row in the Americas, while EMEA also stepped down.

"Consulting revenues are not accelerating and are stuck at +3% y/y cc for the 3rd consecutive quarter. Meanwhile, outsourcing revenues continue to slow down. While the results were solid overall vs [versus] expectations, we don't think the quarter dispels any concerns around AI disruption," said Thind and his team.

Separately, on Accenture's recently announced collaboration with Microsoft (MSFT), UBS said, "we believe this benefits both, as Accenture sees longer client engagements, higher value work, and stronger positioning as an execution partner. Microsoft scales deployment without hiring thousands of service engineers + reduces churn."

Update: The story was updated with analysts' comments.

data.title: Marsh & McLennan to acquire Spain-based AltamarCAM, terms not disclosed
data.description:
Marsh & McLennan (MRSH) on Thursday announced an acquisition of the specialist private markets asset manager AltamarCAM.

With €20B in AUM, the Spain-based AltamarCAM is set to become a part of the Marsh unit Mercer.

Financial terms of the deal were not disclosed.

The transaction is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

data.title: OpenAI plans to acquire startup Astral to boost Codex abilities
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) continues to acquire startups, and the Microsoft-backed (MSFT) company announced today it plans to buy Astral in order to integrate its coding tools into Codex.

Codex is OpenAI's artificial intelligence-powered coding agent, which is also one of its most popular offerings. It has more than 2M active users, and its usage has increased by five times year-to-date, according to OpenAI.

"Astral's tools are used by millions of Python developers," said OpenAI's Codex Lead Thibault Sottiaux. "By bringing their expertise and ecosystem to OpenAI, we're accelerating our vision for Codex as the agent most capable of working across the entire software developer lifecycle." 

Astral builds high-performance developer tools for the Python ecosystem. Its most popular coding tools include Ruff, uv, and ty. The Brooklyn-based company was founded in 2022.

"Today, AI is rapidly changing the way we build software, and the pace of that change is only accelerating," said Astral founder Charlie Marsh. "If our goal is to make programming more productive, then building at the frontier of AI and software feels like the highest-leverage thing we can do."

"It is increasingly clear to me that Codex is that frontier," he added. "And by bringing Astral's tooling and expertise to OpenAI, we're putting ourselves in a position to push it forward. After joining the Codex team, we'll continue building our open source tools, explore ways they can work more seamlessly with Codex, and expand our reach to think more broadly about the future of software development."

Financial details of the proposed acquisition were not revealed. Over the past several months, OpenAI has acquired Promptfoo, Software Applications, and Neptune.

OpenAI's most significant competitors in the AI coding space include Cursor and Anthropic (ANTHRO). Cursor is in talks with investors for a funding round that would value the startup at about $50B. It is used by more than 30,000 developers at Nvidia (NVDA), and its annual revenue run rate has doubled over the last three months.

data.title: We’re not facing ‘stagflation’ despite surge in oil prices – UBS Global Wealth Management’s Paul Donovan
data.description:
The global economy is not facing stagflation despite the recent surge in oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM), according to Paul Donovan, chief economist at UBS Global Wealth Management.

“I don’t think we are facing a stagflationary environment,” Donovan said in an interview with CNBC, noting that current conditions differ significantly from historical periods of stagflation.

Donovan emphasized that the economic starting point today is fundamentally different from the 1970s or even 2022.

“We’re starting with benign inflation pressures, and unemployment remains relatively low in the major economies around the world,” he explained. These factors suggest that underlying inflation pressures will likely remain subdued despite the oil price shock.

The key concern, according to Donovan, lies in the duration of elevated prices rather than the spike itself.

“The world can manage with a higher oil price for a short period of time… but when you go beyond that… that is something which creates more economic damage,” he said.

He noted that some economies have already adapted, pointing to German industry becoming significantly more energy-efficient following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

Central banks are expected to largely ignore the immediate oil shock in favor of focusing on broader economic growth.

Donovan highlighted that the Bank of Canada recently signaled it would “look through the oil prices as a one-off shock” and maintain a bias toward considering growth implications rather than reacting to short-lived inflation spikes.

He expects the Federal Reserve to take a similar approach.

Even if oil prices (CO1:COM) surge to $150 per barrel and remain elevated, the inflationary impact would be temporary, Donovan explained.

Looking ahead, Donovan expressed doubt that oil prices would continue escalating indefinitely.

“The growth of renewables, etcetera, will change demand patterns. We have more demand flexibility than we used to have,” he said. Without significant second-round effects such as changing wage behavior, the Federal Reserve is unlikely to anticipate a persistent inflation story beyond a single year.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Federal Reserve keeps policy on hold for third straight meeting, as Iran war clouds outlook
  • Fed rate cut no longer priced in for 2026
  • Philly Fed Manufacturing Index unexpectedly rises in March
  • Initial jobless claims unexpectedly drop in past week
  • America is now $39T in debt. Is there an end in sight?

data.title: Palantir in spotlight as Mizuho reiterates Outperform after exec meeting
data.description:
• Palantir Technologies (PLTR) was in the spotlight on Thursday as Mizuho Securities reiterated its Outperform rating and $195 price target on the enterprise software company after a meeting with executives. 
  • “We recently hosted meetings with Peter Czimback, Independent Advisor to the PLTR CFO and former VP of Experience Innovation at Aramark (a PLTR customer), along with a current PLTR customer in the Architectural, Engineering and Construction vertical,” analyst Gregg Moskowitz wrote in a note to clients. “During our discussions, the executives highlighted compelling deployment use cases, PLTR's differentiated bespoke approach to enterprise transformation, and the absence of any comparable alternative. We reiterate our view that PLTR is in a category of one, delivering total revenue growth, acceleration, and margin expansion at scale that is unlike anything else in software. We also remain bullish on a surging US Commercial business fueled by rising enterprise adoption of AIP and growing urgency around operationalizing AI. And more broadly, we remain steadfast in our view that PLTR is increasingly well-positioned to benefit from long-term trends in AI, government digital transformation, and industrial modernization.”

data.title: Gas prices are poised to jump as oil climbs on geopolitical risks
data.description:
Escalating tensions in the Middle East are keeping oil (CL1:COM) prices elevated, with spillover effects increasingly visible at the pump for everyday consumers. The ongoing conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel has heightened supply concerns, reinforcing upward pressure across global energy markets.

Retail gasoline prices are beginning to reflect those dynamics, with traders in prediction markets anticipating further gains in the near term. Data from Polymarket indicates a strong consensus toward higher prices by month-end. 

Traders on Polymarket assign just a 4% probability that gasoline will fall to $3.15 per gallon by the end of March, while a dominant 83% expect prices to climb toward $4. 

The sharp skew in expectations underscores growing concern that sustained geopolitical instability could keep energy costs elevated, adding another layer of pressure on household budgets and complicating the broader inflation outlook. 

Outlined below is what Polymarket participants are saying when asked, what will gas hit by the end of March:

  • Above $5.00, 9% chance.
  • Above $4.50, 24% chance.
  • Above $4.25, 57% chance.
  • Above $4.00, 83% chance.
  • Below $3.15, 4% chance.
  • Below $3.10, 3% chance.
  • Below $3.05, 3% chance.
  • Below $3.00, 2% chance. 

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL). 

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC).

data.title: United adds ultra-premium seats to the 787-9s
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) announced on Thursday that it is opening ticket sales for its first Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner featuring the new United Elevated interior, which becomes the carrier’s most premium international aircraft with 99 total premium seats. 

The jet debuts the new United Polaris Studio suites, which are eight lie‑flat, all‑aisle‑access seats that are 25% larger than standard Polaris, with privacy doors, ottomans with seatbelts for a companion in most suites, an Ossetra caviar amuse‑bouche, upgraded amenity kits, wireless charging, Bluetooth audio, and a 27‑inch 4K OLED screen. Across cabins, the elevated interior adds larger 4K screens with Bluetooth, wireless charging in premium cabins, upgraded dining and amenities, larger overhead bins, and more power outlets.

The aircraft will initially operate domestically between San Francisco and Houston before launching international service from San Francisco to Singapore on April 22 and to London from April 30, with United (UAL) targeting at least 30 such 787‑9s in service by the end of 2027.

Of course, United Airlines (UAL) has a long history with the Dreamliner, becoming the first North American airline to take delivery of a 787 in September 2012 and the first U.S. carrier to place the type into commercial service that November. Its inaugural revenue 787 flight operated domestically from Houston to Chicago before the aircraft began flying long‑haul routes to Africa, Asia, and Europe, illustrating United’s early use of the type for both proving and international missions. Notably, United (UAL) has built one of the largest 787 fleets among U.S. carriers and segmented it into different configurations, including premium‑light and now ultra‑premium sub-fleets, to match varying demand profiles.
